name: Guam (United States)

colors.symbolism: Guam's unity with the sea and sky and its ties to the United States
the blood shed by the Chamorro people during the Japanese occupation in World War II
the sandy beaches of Guam (and the Latte Stone outline)
the vegetation, the palm tree, and the prosperity of the land
the coconut palm trunk and the proa canoe

description: A dark blue field bordered by a narrow red stripe on all four sides; centred is the pointed-oval (sling-stone-shaped) Seal of Guam, depicting a coconut palm on a beach beside Hagatna Bay with a flying proa, the Two Lovers' Point cliff, and the inscription 'GUAM'.
history: The current design was adopted by Guam Legislative Act on 9 February 1948. The territorial seal in the centre had been designed earlier (in 1917 by Helen Paul, wife of a U.S. naval officer); a version of the flag with the seal on a dark-blue field was first authorised in 1930. The red border was added in 1948 to commemorate Chamorro lives lost during the Japanese occupation of 1941-1944.

features.symbolism: the dark blue field with a narrow red border encloses the territorial seal.

features.role: Seal of Guam (pointed-oval shape, evoking a Chamorro sling stone)
features.symbolism: an almond-shaped (sling-stone) cartouche outlined in yellow, depicting Hagatna Bay with a beach in the foreground, the Punta Dos Amantes (Two Lovers' Point) cliff on the horizon, a coconut palm growing from the sand, and a flying proa (traditional outrigger canoe) on the water; below, the inscription 'GUAM'. The pointed shape recalls a slingstone, the traditional weapon of the Chamorro.
